planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; yellow African iris is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and requires no supplemental irrigation in zone 8–9 after the first growing season.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow African iris (biscuit plant) is a South African native that produces clumps of stiff, sword-shaped, evergreen leaves and repeatedly blooms with pale-yellow, iris-like flowers distinctly spotted with dark-brown to maroon spots near the flower center, giving each flower the appearance of a painted Japanese screen panel. Unlike bearded irises, the individual flower stalks do not need to be cut after blooming but instead produce additional flowers from the same stem over the course of a season — do not cut the flowering stems until they have completely finished producing buds. Dietes bicolor is slightly more compact and drought-tolerant than the related D. iridioides (white African iris) and performs especially well in the dry, hot conditions of zone 8–11 Gulf Coast and southwestern gardens. Excellent as a low-maintenance evergreen clump in dry, sunny or partially shaded borders, medians, and slopes. Self-seeds modestly. Completely maintenance-free once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade after frost; goldfussia flowers most heavily in autumn when day length shortens and is an excellent companion to fall-blooming chrysanthemums and asters in the partially shaded border.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade; goldfussia is a Thai and Myanmar native shrub that is prized in zone 8–11 gardens for two qualities rare in shade-tolerant woody plants: dramatically iridescent dark-green foliage with a blue-purple metallic sheen in certain light conditions, and a profuse display of soft, powder-blue to violet-blue tubular flowers in autumn and winter — when most garden shrubs have ceased flowering. The combination of semi-glossy, iridescent leaves and late-season blue flowers makes goldfussia an exceptionally valuable plant for providing fall and winter interest in the shaded border. The plant grows as a rounded, mounding shrub with soft, slightly hairy leaves and is somewhat frost-sensitive, typically dying back to the roots in zone 8 winters before resprouting vigorously from the base in spring. In zones 9–11 it grows as a semi-evergreen to evergreen perennial shrub. Excellent combined with autumn-blooming companions in the partially shaded border.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in consistently moist to aquatic conditions in partial shade to full sun in spring; water celery grows most vigorously in shallow water (1–3 inches deep) at pond margins, in rain gardens, and in wet soil beside streams — it requires consistently wet soil and will not thrive in normal garden conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently wet soil, shallow water, or at pond margins in partial shade to full sun; water celery (water parsley, minari, Japanese parsley) is an East Asian aquatic edible herb widely consumed in Korean, Japanese, and Chinese cuisines — fresh young stems and leaves have a crisp texture and fresh, parsley-celery flavor that makes them excellent in Korean naengmyeon (cold noodles), doenjang-jjigae (soybean paste soup), japchae, salads, and as a garnish. It grows naturally in shallow streams, rice paddies, and wet meadows, making it an excellent functional plant for rain gardens, constructed wetlands, and ornamental pond margins where few edible plants thrive. The lacy, finely divided, bright-green foliage creates an ornamental effect at the water's edge, and the plant spreads steadily by stolons in ideal wet conditions to create dense, productive colonies. A variegated cultivar ('Flamingo') with pink, white, and green foliage is grown as an ornamental. Harvest the young stem tips continuously for kitchen use. Avoid consuming the related O. crocata (hemlock water dropwort), which is highly toxic.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; hardy plumbago emerges late in spring — do not assume plants are dead before late May as they are among the last perennials to re-emerge from dormancy. Mark planting locations to avoid accidental removal.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average soil in partial shade to full sun; hardy plumbago is a low-growing, spreading Chinese perennial groundcover that provides two successive ornamental events: a brilliant display of intensely cobalt-blue, five-petaled, phlox-like flowers from late August through October (at a season when true cobalt-blue flowers are extraordinarily rare in the perennial garden), followed immediately by a striking fall foliage display in deep crimson-red as temperatures drop, often with both blue flowers and red foliage present simultaneously for a breathtaking color contrast. The plant is a semi-woody groundcover that spreads steadily by underground stolons to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat. It emerges late in spring (after most perennials are well established), so patience is required after winter dormancy. Excellent as an edging plant, for slope coverage, and in mixed perennial borders where late-season blue is needed. Drought-tolerant once established, performs acceptably in partial shade but blooms most freely in full sun. Not to be confused with tropical Plumbago auriculata (Cape leadwort), which is tender below zone 9.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; white indigoberry is extremely tolerant of salt spray, sandy soil, and coastal conditions — one of the most salt-tolerant native shrubs available for south Florida and Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Direct-sow or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; white indigoberry (milkberry, prick-wood) is a spiny, evergreen native shrub of south Florida, the Caribbean, and coastal tropical Americas that is one of the most reliable and wildlife-valuable small shrubs for zone 9b–12 coastal and dry upland landscapes. Its small, white, fragrant, tubular flowers produce an indigo-blue to white, waxy berry that is a critical food source for mockingbirds, thrashers, bluebirds, and over thirty additional bird species — making it one of the most bird-attractive native shrubs of south Florida. The sharp, paired spines make it an excellent barrier hedge for security plantings, and the plant's extreme tolerance of salt spray, drought, and poor sandy soil makes it ideally suited to the harsh conditions of coastal landscapes where few other shrubs thrive. Slow-growing to 3–10 feet, it can be used as a hedge, a natural barrier, or allowed to grow as a specimen with an attractive, irregular branch structure. Largely absent from commercial nurseries but available from native plant nurseries.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
